learningOutcomes: Define displacement, velocity, and acceleration with correct units
Distinguish between scalar and vector quantities
Interpret distance-time and speed-time graphs
realLifeContexts: Reading a car speedometer versus a GPS displacement tracker
Comparing distance run versus displacement in a circular track
Interpreting a bus journey on a distance-time graph
commonMisconceptions: Speed and velocity are the same quantity
A negative velocity means the object has stopped

learningOutcomes: Apply the equations of motion (SUVAT) to solve problems
Analyse projectile motion by resolving into horizontal and vertical components
Apply Newton's Laws to systems with multiple forces
Calculate resultant forces and use free-body diagrams
realLifeContexts: Calculating the range of a football kicked at an angle
Designing crash barriers using force and deceleration principles
Analysing the forces on a lift (elevator) accelerating upwards
Modelling the trajectory of a basketball shot
commonMisconceptions: The horizontal and vertical components of projectile motion affect each other
A heavier projectile always travels further than a lighter one

learningOutcomes: Derive and apply the equations of motion from calculus principles
Analyse circular motion and centripetal acceleration
Apply the impulse-momentum theorem to collision problems
Evaluate the concept of frames of reference and relative motion
realLifeContexts: Satellite orbital mechanics and centripetal force
Crumple zone engineering: extending collision time to reduce force
Relativistic effects in GPS satellite time correction
Banked road design for safe high-speed cornering
commonMisconceptions: Centripetal force is a special additional force in circular systems
In an inelastic collision, momentum is not conserved

learningOutcomes: Define work done as force multiplied by displacement in the direction of the force
State the principle of conservation of energy
Calculate gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy
realLifeContexts: Calculating the energy needed to climb a flight of stairs
Comparing the power output of a cyclist and a car engine
Why a falling object speeds up as potential energy converts to kinetic
commonMisconceptions: Work is done whenever a force is applied, even if no displacement occurs
Energy is destroyed when it is transferred to heat

learningOutcomes: Apply the work-energy theorem to solve problems
Calculate efficiency of energy transfers
Analyse elastic and inelastic collisions using energy principles
Solve problems involving power, including variable power
realLifeContexts: Calculating the efficiency of a car engine from fuel energy to motion
Designing regenerative braking systems in electric vehicles
Analysing a bungee jump in terms of energy transformations
Comparing the power of athletes in different sports
commonMisconceptions: A perfectly elastic collision conserves kinetic energy and momentum equally
Efficiency can exceed 100% with a clever enough design

learningOutcomes: Apply the first and second laws of thermodynamics to mechanical systems
Calculate work done by variable forces using integration
Evaluate energy losses in real systems and propose improvements
Analyse gravitational potential energy in orbital mechanics
realLifeContexts: Designing a more efficient steam turbine for a power station
Calculating the escape velocity from Earth's gravitational field
Optimising a roller coaster track for minimum energy loss
Analysing the Carnot cycle efficiency of ideal heat engines
commonMisconceptions: Gravitational potential energy is always a positive quantity
The second law of thermodynamics only applies to heat engines

learningOutcomes: Describe the properties of waves: amplitude, wavelength, frequency, and speed
Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves
Explain how sound travels as a longitudinal wave through a medium
realLifeContexts: Why sound does not travel in a vacuum (space)
How a guitar string produces sound waves in air
Why ships use sonar to map the ocean floor
prerequisites: Basic understanding of vibration and energy
commonMisconceptions: All waves require a medium to travel through
Sound waves move particles along with them permanently

learningOutcomes: Apply the wave equation v = fλ to solve problems
Explain reflection, refraction, diffraction, and interference
Analyse standing waves and resonance in strings and pipes
Calculate intensity and use the decibel scale
realLifeContexts: How noise-cancelling headphones use destructive interference
The design of concert halls to optimise sound resonance
How ultrasound imaging works in medical diagnostics
Why light bends around the edge of an obstacle (diffraction)
commonMisconceptions: Diffraction only occurs with light, not sound
Constructive interference always makes the wave louder

learningOutcomes: Apply the Doppler effect to calculate frequency shifts
Analyse thin film interference and diffraction grating patterns
Evaluate the wave model versus the particle model of light
Apply superposition principle to complex wave interactions
realLifeContexts: Police speed radar using the Doppler effect
Anti-reflection coatings on camera lenses using thin film interference
Spectroscopy: analysing starlight to determine composition
Holography and its uses in security holograms
commonMisconceptions: The Doppler effect only applies to sound waves
Coherent sources must always be in phase with each other

learningOutcomes: State the laws of reflection and apply them to plane mirrors
Explain refraction using the concept of change in wave speed
Describe image formation by concave and convex lenses
realLifeContexts: How spectacles (glasses) correct vision defects
Why a straw appears bent in a glass of water
How a magnifying glass creates an enlarged image
commonMisconceptions: Refraction and reflection are the same process
A concave lens always forms a real image

learningOutcomes: Use the lens formula 1/f = 1/v − 1/u to locate images
Calculate magnification and relate it to object/image distances
Explain total internal reflection and its applications
Analyse dispersion of white light through a prism
realLifeContexts: How optical fibres transmit data using total internal reflection
Designing a telescope or microscope using lens systems
How a camera focuses light onto a sensor
Diamond cutting to maximise internal reflection and brilliance
commonMisconceptions: Total internal reflection can occur when light travels from any medium
A real image can always be seen without a screen

learningOutcomes: Analyse optical instruments (telescope, microscope, eye) using ray diagrams
Apply Snell's law to multi-layer refraction problems
Evaluate the wave nature of light through polarisation and interference
Explain the limits of optical resolution (Rayleigh criterion)
realLifeContexts: Polarised sunglasses reducing glare from reflective surfaces
Astronomy: limits on telescope resolution due to diffraction
3D cinema using polarised light
Fibre optic communication bandwidth and data capacity
commonMisconceptions: Polarisation proves light is a particle
Larger telescope aperture always gives better image quality

learningOutcomes: Describe the two types of electric charge and the law of attraction/repulsion
Explain charging by friction, contact, and induction
Describe the electric field around point charges and between parallel plates
realLifeContexts: Clothes clinging together from static electricity in a dryer
A Van de Graaff generator making hair stand on end
Lightning as discharge between clouds and Earth
commonMisconceptions: Only insulators can become charged
Electric field lines show the direction of force on a negative charge

learningOutcomes: Apply Coulomb's Law to calculate the force between point charges
Define and calculate electric potential and potential difference
Analyse the behaviour of conductors and insulators in electric fields
Calculate the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor
realLifeContexts: Touchscreen technology using capacitive sensing
Defibrillators storing and discharging electrical energy via capacitors
Electrostatic precipitators removing particles from industrial exhaust
Inkjet printer heads using electrostatic deflection
commonMisconceptions: Electric potential and electric field are the same quantity
A capacitor stores charge permanently regardless of the circuit

learningOutcomes: Apply Gauss's Law to calculate electric fields for symmetric charge distributions
Analyse energy stored in capacitors and capacitor networks
Derive the relationship between electric field and potential using calculus
Evaluate the behaviour of dielectrics and their effect on capacitance
realLifeContexts: DRAM memory chips using capacitors to store binary data
High-voltage power line design to manage electric fields
MRI machines using capacitor-based RF pulse generation
Supercapacitors for energy storage in rapid-charge devices
commonMisconceptions: Gauss's Law only applies to spherical charge distributions
Dielectrics increase capacitance by reducing the charge on the plates

learningOutcomes: Describe the magnetic field pattern around a current-carrying wire and solenoid
State that a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force
Use Fleming's Left-Hand Rule to determine the direction of the force
realLifeContexts: How an electric motor converts electrical energy to rotation
How a loudspeaker uses the motor effect to produce sound
Maglev trains using electromagnetic levitation
prerequisites: Magnetic field of a bar magnet
commonMisconceptions: The magnetic force on a current acts in the direction of the current
Electromagnets and permanent magnets behave identically

learningOutcomes: Apply F = BIL and F = Bqv to calculate magnetic forces
Explain electromagnetic induction using Faraday's and Lenz's Laws
Analyse the operation of AC and DC generators
Explain the principle and efficiency of transformers
realLifeContexts: Power stations generating AC electricity using induction
Step-up and step-down transformers in the national grid
Induction hobs heating pots without direct flame
Wireless phone charging using electromagnetic induction
commonMisconceptions: Lenz's Law means the induced current opposes all currents in the circuit
A transformer works with DC as well as AC

learningOutcomes: Apply Ampère's Law to calculate magnetic fields for symmetric current distributions
Analyse AC circuits with inductors, capacitors, and resistors (LCR circuits)
Derive and apply Maxwell's equations conceptually
Evaluate electromagnetic wave propagation and its relationship to light
realLifeContexts: Radio tuning circuits using LCR resonance to select frequencies
MRI machines using strong magnetic fields and RF pulses
Wireless power transmission in industrial applications
Fibre optic and microwave communication using EM wave properties
commonMisconceptions: Maxwell's equations are only relevant to advanced physics research
Impedance and resistance are interchangeable in AC circuits

learningOutcomes: Define temperature, thermal energy, and heat
Describe the three methods of heat transfer: conduction, convection, and radiation
Explain thermal expansion in solids, liquids, and gases
realLifeContexts: Why metal feels colder than wood at the same temperature
How a central heating system uses convection to warm a room
Gaps in railway tracks to allow thermal expansion
commonMisconceptions: Temperature and thermal energy (heat) are the same thing
Heat travels only upwards because hot air rises

learningOutcomes: Calculate heat transfer using Q = mcΔT
Explain latent heat and apply it to changes of state
Apply the ideal gas laws to solve thermodynamic problems
Describe the thermodynamic processes: isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric
realLifeContexts: Why coastal regions have milder climates than inland areas (water's high SHC)
How a refrigerator uses latent heat to cool its contents
Diesel engine cycle as an adiabatic compression process
How a pressure cooker raises the boiling point of water
commonMisconceptions: An adiabatic process has constant temperature
An ideal gas has no intermolecular forces, so no energy is stored within it

learningOutcomes: Apply the second law of thermodynamics and the concept of entropy
Analyse thermodynamic cycles (Carnot, Otto, diesel) for efficiency
Apply statistical mechanics to link microscopic behaviour to macroscopic properties
Evaluate real engine performance against theoretical maximum efficiency
realLifeContexts: Why no heat engine ever achieves 100% efficiency
Designing combined heat and power (CHP) plants to maximise useful energy output
Liquid nitrogen cooling in industrial processes and medical applications
Climate engineering proposals evaluated through thermodynamic limits
commonMisconceptions: Entropy is a measure of disorder only, with no quantitative definition
The Carnot engine is a practical design goal rather than a theoretical limit

learningOutcomes: Describe the structure of the atom: protons, neutrons, and electrons
Explain radioactive decay and identify alpha, beta, and gamma radiation
State the difference between fission and fusion reactions
realLifeContexts: Smoke detectors using alpha radiation from americium
Carbon-14 dating to determine the age of archaeological artefacts
Nuclear power stations using fission to generate electricity
commonMisconceptions: Radioactive materials are always dangerous to be near
Fission and fusion are opposite processes that cancel each other out

learningOutcomes: Apply the photoelectric effect to explain the particle nature of light
Perform half-life calculations for radioactive decay
Explain nuclear binding energy and mass defect
Describe wave-particle duality and the de Broglie hypothesis
realLifeContexts: Solar cells converting photons to electrical energy (photoelectric principle)
Radiation therapy using targeted gamma rays to destroy tumour cells
PET scans using positron emission for medical imaging
Electron microscopes using de Broglie wavelength for nanoscale imaging
commonMisconceptions: Brighter light always causes the photoelectric effect regardless of frequency
Half-life means exactly half the atoms decay in that time period

learningOutcomes: Apply Heisenberg's uncertainty principle to quantum systems
Analyse energy levels and spectral lines using the quantum model of the atom
Evaluate the principles behind nuclear reactors and fusion energy research
Apply special relativity to mass-energy equivalence (E = mc²)
realLifeContexts: Laser technology in telecommunications, surgery, and manufacturing
ITER fusion reactor as a future clean energy source
GPS satellites requiring relativistic time correction
Quantum computing exploiting superposition of quantum states
commonMisconceptions: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle is due to imprecise measurement instruments
E = mc² means any object can be converted entirely to energy easily
